# DFS structure but no visited check - infinite loop on cycles
def dfs(graph, start):
    visited, stack = [], [start]
    safety = 0
    while stack and safety < 1000:
        safety += 1
        nxt = stack.pop()
        visited.append(nxt)
        for vertex in reversed(graph[nxt]):
            stack.append(vertex)
    return visited

# Builds an adjacency list but never traverses it; returns 0.
def calc(N, A):
    graph = [[] for _ in range(N + 1)]
    for edge in A:
        start, end = edge
        graph[start].append(end)
    return 0

# Iterates the recurrence linearly; works only for small n.
def calc(n):
    a = 0
    c = 1000000007
    for i in range(n):
        a = (2 * a + 1) % c
    return a

# Does not use f; runs an empty narrowing loop and returns l.
def calc(f):
    l = 0
    r = 1000000000
    while l < r:
        m = (l + r) // 2
        l = m + 1
    return l

# Returns an array of zeros; no prefix sum computation.
def calc(a, b):
    n = len(a)
    d = [0] * n
    for i in range(n):
        d[i] = 0
    return d

# Builds an adjacency list but never traverses it; returns 0.
def calc(N, A):
    graph = [[] for _ in range(N + 1)]
    for edge in A:
        start, end = edge
        graph[start].append(end)
    return 0

Let N be the length of array a.

Method f
Part A: int n = a.length; int s = 0; - constant-time initialisation.
Part B (nested loops over i, j): the inner body executes for every (i, j) with 0 <= j <= i < N.
Part C: int t = g(a); return s + t; - one call to g plus a constant-time return.

Parts A and the return in Part C are Theta(1).

Part B (nested loops):
- Combined iterations: when i = 0 the inner loop runs once; when i = 1 it runs twice; ...; when i = N-1 it runs N times. Total = 1 + 2 + ... + N = N(N+1)/2 = Theta(N^2).
- Cost per iteration: Theta(1) - two array reads, one multiplication, one += assignment; the loop checks and increments are Theta(1).
- Total cost of Part B: Theta(N^2) iterations x Theta(1) = Theta(N^2).

Part C:
- The call g(a) costs Theta(log N) (see g below); the addition and return are Theta(1).
- Total cost of Part C: Theta(log N).

Overall running time of f:
Theta(1) (Part A) + Theta(N^2) (Part B) + Theta(log N) (Part C) = Theta(N^2).

Method g
Part D: int n = a.length; int t = 0; int k = n; - constant-time initialisations.
Part E (while loop): each iteration accesses a[k-1] and halves k.
Part F: return t; - constant time.

Parts D and F are Theta(1).

Part E:
- Iterations: k starts at N and is replaced by k/2 each iteration, so the loop runs Theta(log N) times until k becomes 0.
- Cost per iteration: Theta(1) - one array read, one += assignment, one integer division, one comparison.
- Total cost of Part E: Theta(log N) x Theta(1) = Theta(log N).

Overall running time of g:
Theta(1) + Theta(log N) + Theta(1) = Theta(log N).

f has two nested loops and then calls g. The setup statements are constant.

The nested loops in f iterate roughly N^2 times in total because they are two nested loops over the array of length N. Each iteration of the inner body performs an array read, a multiplication and an addition into s; these are all O(1) since they are simple operations. So the nested loops cost Theta(N^2) * Theta(1) = Theta(N^2).

Then f calls g, which adds some extra cost from g.

g has a while loop that runs Theta(log N) times. Each iteration of g does an array access, an addition and a division, all of which are Theta(1). Total cost of the loop: Theta(log N) * Theta(1) = Theta(log N).

Overall, g runs in Theta(log N) and f runs in Theta(N^2).

The function f has a loop, so it runs in O(N) time. Inside the loop body it performs some small operations like adding and multiplying values from the array. The g method does some halving thing and looks small. The answer is f = O(N) and g = O(1).

Let M be the length of array a and N be the length of array b.

Method f
Part A: int m = a.length; int s = 0; - constant time.
Part B (for loop): iterates i over 0..M-1, calling g(b, a[i]) each time.
Part C: return s; - constant time.

Parts A and C are Theta(1).

Part B:
- Iterations: i runs from 0 to M-1, so the loop has exactly Theta(M) iterations.
- Cost per iteration: dominated by the call g(b, a[i]); since g is a binary search over b it costs Theta(log N). The comparison and conditional += are Theta(1). Per iteration: Theta(log N).
- Total cost of Part B: Theta(M) x Theta(log N) = Theta(M log N).

Overall running time of f:
Theta(1) + Theta(M log N) + Theta(1) = Theta(M log N).

Method g
Part D: int l = 0; int r = b.length - 1; - constant time.
Part E (while loop): standard binary search.
Part F: return -1; - constant time.

Parts D and F are Theta(1).

Part E:
- Iterations: each iteration either returns immediately or replaces [l, r] with one of its halves. The interval size halves each iteration, so the loop runs at most Theta(log N) times.
- Cost per iteration: Theta(1) - midpoint computation, two array accesses, two comparisons, one update of l or r.
- Total cost of Part E: Theta(log N) x Theta(1) = Theta(log N).

Overall running time of g:
Theta(1) + Theta(log N) + Theta(1) = Theta(log N).

f has a single for loop that iterates over array a. The loop runs M times because i goes from 0 to m. Inside the loop f calls g(b, a[i]).

The body of f's loop has a call to g, plus a comparison and an addition. The g call presumably takes log N or so since it looks like a binary search. So f's loop cost per iteration depends on g.

f total cost ~ M * cost_of_g.

g uses a while loop with mid = (l + r) / 2. It runs log N times. Each iteration just does a few comparisons and updates, which is constant. So g is around log N.

Overall: g = Theta(log N), f = Theta(M log N).
f loops over array a so it is O(M). g uses some indices and runs through array b. Both functions perform simple operations inside their loops. The total time is roughly linear in M and N.

Let N be the side length of matrix mat (mat is N x N).

Method f
Part A: int n = mat.length; int s = 0; - constant time.
Part B (nested loops over i, j): inner loop runs from j = i to j = N-1.
Part C: int t = g(mat); return s + t; - one call to g and constant work.

Parts A and the return in Part C are Theta(1).

Part B (nested loops):
- Combined iterations: for each i, j runs from i to N-1 giving (N - i) iterations. Total = N + (N-1) + ... + 1 = N(N+1)/2 = Theta(N^2).
- Cost per iteration: Theta(1) - one matrix read mat[i][j] and one += assignment, plus loop checks and increments.
- Total cost of Part B: Theta(N^2) x Theta(1) = Theta(N^2).

Part C:
- Cost of g is Theta(N) (see g below); the addition and return are Theta(1).
- Total cost of Part C: Theta(N).

Overall running time of f:
Theta(1) (Part A) + Theta(N^2) (Part B) + Theta(N) (Part C) = Theta(N^2).

Method g
Part D: int n = mat.length; int t = 0; - constant time.
Part E (for loop along the diagonal): single loop over i from 0 to N-1.
Part F: return t; - constant time.

Parts D and F are Theta(1).

Part E:
- Iterations: the loop runs i from 0 to N-1, giving Theta(N) iterations.
- Cost per iteration: Theta(1) - one matrix access mat[i][i] and one += assignment.
- Total cost of Part E: Theta(N) x Theta(1) = Theta(N).

Overall running time of g:
Theta(1) + Theta(N) + Theta(1) = Theta(N).

f has two nested loops and then calls g. The setup statements outside the loops are constant.

The nested loops sweep over part of the matrix; together they run Theta(N^2) times since it is essentially a double loop on an N x N matrix. Each iteration just reads a matrix entry and accumulates into s, which is Theta(1). So the nested loops cost Theta(N^2) * Theta(1) = Theta(N^2).

f then calls g, which adds some cost from g.

g has a single for loop that runs Theta(N) times (along the diagonal). Each iteration is Theta(1) since it just reads one matrix entry and adds. So g's loop is Theta(N) * Theta(1) = Theta(N).

Overall, g is Theta(N) and f is Theta(N^2).
f loops through the matrix so it takes O(N) time. The g function reads the diagonal which is just a few values. Total time is approximately linear.

Let N be the length of array a.

Method f
Part A: int n = a.length; int s = 0; int k = 1; - constant time.
Part B (while loop): condition k <= n, the body calls g(a, k) and doubles k.
Part C: return s; - constant time.

Parts A and C are Theta(1).

Part B (loop):
- Iterations: k starts at 1 and is multiplied by 2 each iteration. The loop continues while k <= N, so k reaches the bound after about log2(N) doublings. Number of iterations: Theta(log N).
- Cost per iteration: dominated by the call g(a, k), which is Theta(N) (a full linear scan of a; see g below). The doubling and += are Theta(1). Per iteration: Theta(N).
- Total cost of Part B: Theta(log N) x Theta(N) = Theta(N log N).

Overall running time of f:
Theta(1) + Theta(N log N) + Theta(1) = Theta(N log N).

Method g
Part D: int t = 0; - constant time.
Part E (for loop): scans the entire array a regardless of x.
Part F: return t; - constant time.

Parts D and F are Theta(1).

Part E:
- Iterations: the loop runs i from 0 to a.length - 1, giving Theta(N) iterations.
- Cost per iteration: Theta(1) - one array read, one comparison, possibly one += assignment.
- Total cost of Part E: Theta(N) x Theta(1) = Theta(N).

Overall running time of g:
Theta(1) + Theta(N) + Theta(1) = Theta(N).

f has a while loop that uses k as a counter. The loop runs Theta(log N) times since k changes by a factor each iteration. The body calls g, so each iteration's cost depends on g.

If g runs in Theta(N), then f's loop costs roughly Theta(log N) * cost_per_iter.

g has a for loop over a, so it iterates Theta(N) times. Each iteration of g does a constant amount of work - one array access, a comparison, maybe an add. So g's loop is Theta(N) * Theta(1) = Theta(N).

So f is Theta(N log N) and g is Theta(N).
f keeps multiplying k by 2 so it runs forever or a lot. Inside the body it calls g which does something on the array. The functions probably take linear time overall. f = O(N) and g = O(N).

Let N be the number of nodes in the linked list with head h.

Method f
Part A: int s = 0; Node p = h; - constant time.
Part B (while loop): traverses the list, calling g(p) at each node.
Part C: return s; - constant time.

Parts A and C are Theta(1).

Part B (loop):
- Iterations: p starts at h and is advanced by p = p.next in each iteration, until it becomes null after visiting all N nodes. Theta(N) iterations.
- Cost per iteration: dominated by the call g(p). In the worst case g scans from p to the end of the list - when p = h this is N nodes, when p is the i-th node it is N - i nodes. The worst-case per-iteration cost is Theta(N).
- Total cost of Part B: summing exact costs gives N + (N-1) + ... + 1 = N(N+1)/2 = Theta(N^2). Equivalently, Theta(N) iterations x Theta(N) worst-case per iteration = Theta(N^2).

Overall running time of f:
Theta(1) + Theta(N^2) + Theta(1) = Theta(N^2).

Method g
Part D: int c = 0; Node q = start; - constant time.
Part E (while loop): traverses from start to the end of the list.
Part F: return c; - constant time.

Parts D and F are Theta(1).

Part E:
- Iterations: q starts at start and advances by q.next each iteration until q becomes null. In the worst case (start = h) the loop visits all N nodes, giving Theta(N) iterations.
- Cost per iteration: Theta(1) - one field access q.v, one += assignment, one pointer advance, one null check.
- Total cost of Part E: Theta(N) x Theta(1) = Theta(N).

Overall running time of g:
Theta(1) + Theta(N) + Theta(1) = Theta(N).

f walks through the linked list using p = p.next. Since the list has N nodes, the outer loop runs about N times. The body calls g(p) which traverses the rest of the list.

So the cost per iteration of f is some traversal cost. Multiplying gives roughly N times the cost of g.

g is a while loop that goes through the linked list. It visits Theta(N) nodes in the worst case (when called with start = h). Each step is a constant amount of work - it reads q.v and follows q.next, both of which are Theta(1). Total cost of g's loop: Theta(N) * Theta(1) = Theta(N).

So f is Theta(N^2) and g is Theta(N).
f walks through nodes of the linked list which takes linear time. g also walks the list and counts something. Both functions are O(N). The answer is f = O(N) and g = O(N).
